claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me start by asking you some basic information about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child?** (Or if you have multiple children you're asking about, please tell me their ages)

The age of the child is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please share your child's age (or the ages of all children you're inquiring about).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my daughter Alex - she's 6 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! So you're asking about your daughter Alex who is 6 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, she falls into the automatic qualification age category. Now let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since she's only 6 years old, I assume she's not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment. Can you confirm that Alex is not working 24 or more hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not working at all - she's only 6! She's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - she's in primary school. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in her own right?**

This refers to benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in the child's own name. For a 6-year-old in primary school, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm - is Alex receiving any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in her own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she doesn't receive any benefits in her own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Again, since she's only 6 years old and in primary school, she wouldn't be in any kind of apprenticeship. Can you confirm that Alex is not in an apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, she's not in an apprenticeship - she's just 6 and in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important eligibility requirement.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - you're living abroad at the moment. Let me ask a follow-up question to make sure we've covered all possibilities:

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant (such as working for the UK government, armed forces, or diplomatic service), or are you in certain qualifying countries?**

This is important because people working for the Crown abroad, or in specific circumstances in certain countries, may still qualify for Child Benefit even though they're not physically living in the UK.

Could you tell me:
1. Why are you living abroad? (e.g., work, personal reasons, etc.)
2. What country are you currently living in?
3. Are you working for the UK government, armed forces, or in any official UK capacity abroad?

This will help me determine if you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm living abroad for personal reasons, not for any government work or anything like that. I'm not a Crown servant or working for the armed forces.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and you're not a Crown servant or working in an official UK capacity, and you don't meet any of the other residency paths (like normally living in the UK or having EU Settlement Scheme status), unfortunately you don't meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result: **NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

### Why you're not eligible:

While Alex meets all the child-related requirements (she's 6 years old, in primary school, not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, and not in an apprenticeship), **you don't meet the UK residency requirements**.

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with qualifying conditions)
  - Being a Crown servant living abroad
  - Living in certain qualifying countries

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and don't meet any of these special circumstances, you cannot claim Child Benefit.

### What you could consider:

- **If you return to the UK**: Once you normally live in the UK again, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex (assuming all other criteria remain met)
- **If your circumstances change**: If you start working for the UK government/armed forces abroad, you may become eligible

I understand this may be disappointing news. If you have any questions or if your circumstances change in the future, please don't hesitate to reach out.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old and meets the child-related requirements (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, and not in an apprenticeship). However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, does not normally live in the UK, and does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Since the residency requirement is mandatory for Child Benefit eligibility, the claim for Alex cannot be approved.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is asking about Child Benefit for their 6-year-old daughter Alex. While Alex meets all the child-related eligibility criteria, the claimant fails the mandatory UK residency requirement as they are living abroad for personal reasons and do not meet any of the special circumstances that would allow them to claim while outside the UK.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, does not normally live in the UK, and does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Since the residency requirement is mandatory for Child Benefit eligibility, the claim for Alex cannot be approved.
